content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 15, 1988

Dorienne Hodge
811 Geary St., #411
San Francisco, CA 94109

Re: Our File No. I-88-219

Dear Ms. Hodge:

We have received your letter concerning the June 7, 1988
election.

In your letter, you complained that the ballot pamphlet
contained various opinions concerning the initiative measures.
These opinions are required by state law, which provides that
the ballot pamphlet shall contain a copy of the arguments and
rebuttals for and against each state initiative measure.
(Government Code Sections 88001(c) and 88002(c).) The ballot
pamphlet also must contain an impartial summary of each
measure, prepared by the Legislative Analyst. (Government Code
Section 88003.)

The Fair Political Practices Commission has no jurisdiction
over the other matters discussed in your letter, such as voter
registration, poll workers, or the content of media reporting.
Accordingly, we have referred your letter to the Secretary of

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Dorienne Hodge
June 15, 1988
Page -2-


State's office and to the Registrar of Voters of the City and
County of San Francisco, who have responsibility for the
conduct of elections.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Kathryn E. Donovan
Counsel, Legal Division

811 GEARY ST. #411
SAN FRANCISCO,CA 94109
7 JUNE 1988

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICE COMMISSION
528 J STREET, SUITE 800
P.O. BOX 810
SACRAMENTO,CA 95804

DEAR PERSON;
    YESTERDAY I WENT TO VOTE AT 525
HYDE ST. TO VOTE. THE PRECINCT WAS
NUMBER 4867. I WAS GIVEN A NON-PARTISAN
BALLOT TO VOTE WITH BECAUSE I DECLINED
TO STATE THE PARTY OF MY PREFERENCE
ON MY VOTER'S REGISTRATION CARD.
    ACTUALLY I DO NOT HAVE A PARTY OF
PREFERENCE. I AM A RATIONAL MINDED
PERSON WHO MAKES DECISIONS ON
INDIVIDUALS RATHER THAN THE PARTY
THEY REPRESENT.
    LAST YEAR I REGISTERED TO VOTE
DECLARING THE REPUBLICAN PARTY AND
I DID NOT RECIEVE A VOTER'S REGISTRA-
TION CARD. THE CARD FOR AFFIDAVIT OF
REGISTRATION WAS RETURNED REQUEST-
ING ADDITIONAL INFORMATION. I RETURNED
THE CARD BUT NEVER RECIEVED A
VOTERS REGISTRATION CARD.
    ON THE AFFIDAVIT OF REGISTRATION

3.

OBVIOUSLY EXAMINED THEM BEFORE AND
IF THEY PUT THEM IN THE BALLOT BOX.
     PLEASE DO SOMETHING ABOUT THESE
DICTATORS AT THE POLLS.
     PERHAPS YOU CAN USE YOUR POSITION
TO STOP THE MEDIA FROM REPORTING THEIR
BIASED OPINIONS OF CANDIDATES AND
INITIATIVES. THEY ARE REPORTING THEIR
OPINIONS RATHER THAN THE FACTS.
     THE VOTERS PAMPHLET FROM THE
SECRETARY OF STATE WAS PITIFUL THIS
YEAR. IT GAVE THE OPINIONS OF OTHER
PEOPLE RATHER THAN STATING WHAT THE
INITIATIVES ACTUALLY WERE.
     I HOPE YOU DO SOMETHING SOON. ITS
GETTING HARDER TO DIFFERENTIATE
BETWEEN A DEMOCRACY AND COMMUNISM.


                    sincerely yours;
                    Lorienne Lodge

Office of the Secretary of State
March Fong Eu

1230 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

ELECTIONS DIVISION
(916) 445-0820

For Hearing and Speech Impaired
Only:
(800) 833-8683

June 27, 1988

Dorienne Hodge
811 Geary Street, #411
San Francisco, California 94109

Dear Ms. Hodge:

Your June 7, 1988 letter to the Fair Political Practices
Commission has been referred to Secretary of State March Fong Eu
in her capacity as California's chief elections officer.
Secretary Eu has asked me to respond.

In your letter you expressed displeasure at being denied a
partisan (political party) ballot in the June 7, Primary Election.
As you may now know, voters who "decline to state" their party
affiliation at the time they register are considered by law to be
nonpartisan voters. All voter registration cards so advise
registrants in the instruction portion of the card which
corresponds to information blanks on the form. For your infor-
mation, I enclose a voter registration card with the explanation
regarding political party status highlighted in yellow.

In addition, you were concerned that pollworkers deposited
your ballot in the ballot box for you. This is the procedure
required by California Elections Code section 14246 which states:

"Having folded the ballot, the voter shall deliver
it folded to a member of the precinct board, who shall
then separate the slip containing the number from the
ballot, hand the slip to the voter, and deposit the
ballot in the ballot box in the presence of the voter."

Finally, you noted, again with displeasure, the opinions of
proponents and opponents of ballot measures in the state ballot
pamphlet. These ballot arguments are a required portion of the
ballot pamphlet and are considered by many voters to be a useful
guide to the pros and cons of complicated measures.

Should you wish to seek changes to these election laws, I
would advise you to contact your local representatives to
the State Legislature. Perhaps they would be willing to

Letter to Ms. Hodge
June 27, 1988
Page 2


sponsor appropriate legislative solutions to the problems
you have encountered.

     I hope this information is helpful.  Please feel free to
contact me again if you have elections-related questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        DEBORAH SEILER
                                        Assistant to the Secretary of State
                                        Elections and Political Reform

DS/jm

Enclosure

bcc:  Kathryn E. Donovan

sections.conclusion: The Fair Political Practices Commission has no jurisdiction over the matters discussed in the letter, such as voter registration, poll workers, or media reporting content. The letter has been referred to the Secretary of State's office and the Registrar of Voters. The ballot pamphlet requirements are mandated by state law (Government Code sections 88001(c), 88002(c), and 88003).
sections.question_synthetic: The complainant raised concerns about the June 7, 1988 election, including issues with ballot pamphlet content, voter registration procedures, poll worker practices, and media reporting.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC lacks jurisdiction over the complainant's concerns regarding voter registration, poll procedures, and media content, and has referred the matter to appropriate election authorities. The ballot pamphlet's inclusion of arguments and rebuttals is a state law requirement.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two letters: primary FPPC advice letter declining jurisdiction, and secondary Secretary of State response addressing the actual complaint details. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections marked with headers. The FPPC response is brief and jurisdictional in nature.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The complainant raised concerns about the June 7, 1988 election, including issues with ballot pamphlet content, voter registration procedures, poll worker practices, and media reporting.

CONCLUSION: The Fair Political Practices Commission has no jurisdiction over the matters discussed in the letter, such as voter registration, poll workers, or media reporting content. The letter has been referred to the Secretary of State's office and the Registrar of Voters. The ballot pamphlet requirements are mandated by state law (Government Code sections 88001(c), 88002(c), and 88003).

embedding.summary: This document contains an FPPC response letter (June 15, 1988) declining jurisdiction over a complainant's election-related concerns and referring the matter to the Secretary of State, followed by a response from the Secretary of State's Elections Division (June 27, 1988) addressing the specific complaints.
